column4: Tighten a .jd body to a minimal, useful brief. Use when a .jd reads wordy, restates its frontmatter, or explains the runner instead of the tool.
Open an agent review of the current PR. Use when the user asks to review, critique, or sanity-check a pull request.
Summarize a file or diff for the user. Use when the user asks "what does this do", "summarize", or "explain this file".
Compress or decompress a single file with gzip, zstd, or xz — the stream compressors behind .gz / .zst / .xz. Use to shrink one file (or feed a tar stream), not to bundle many files.
Archiving vs compression, and which tool reads which extension — .tar.gz/.tgz, .tar.zst, .tar.xz, .zip. Pull when choosing a format or deciding between tar, zip, and the stream compressors.
Bundle files into a tar archive and read them back — create (optionally gzip), list contents, extract to a directory, append more files. Use for the unix .tar / .tar.gz / .tgz family.
Build and read .zip archives — create recursively, list contents, extract to a directory, add files to an existing zip. Use for the cross-platform .zip format that Windows and macOS open natively.

The docker CLI surface — its object model (image, container, volume, network), the run-time flags every command shares, and how name-vs-id targeting works. Pull when wiring any docker command or deciding which object a verb acts on.
The container lifecycle — run, list, exec into, stop, remove, and restart containers. Use to start a container from an image, see what's running, get a shell inside one, or stop and clean one up.
The image lifecycle — build from a Dockerfile, pull, push, tag, list, and remove images. Use to build an image, fetch one from a registry, retag it, publish it, or clean images off disk.

Observe a running container — follow logs, inspect its full config as JSON, watch live resource stats, and see its in-container processes. Use to debug why a container misbehaves without changing anything.
Manage docker networks — list, create, inspect, remove, and connect a container to one. Use to wire containers together on a private network or to see and clean up existing networks.

Reclaim disk by pruning unused docker objects — the whole system, or just stopped containers, dangling images, unused volumes, or the build cache. Use to free space, but only when the removed objects are truly disposable.
Manage named volumes — list, create, inspect, and remove the persistent storage containers mount. Use to set up storage that outlives a container, see what volumes exist, or reclaim one.
How the gh CLI is driven — auth, repo targeting, and scriptable JSON output. The shared surface the gh tools build on; pull when wiring any gh command or parsing its output.
Manage GitHub issues — create, list, view, comment, close, edit. Use to file an issue, triage or inspect the queue, update labels/assignees, comment, or close.
Manage GitHub pull requests end to end — create, list, view, checkout, merge, review, comment. Use to open a PR, inspect or check out one, leave a review or comment, or merge.
Manage GitHub Releases — create, list, view, upload assets, download assets. Use to publish a release for a tag, attach or fetch build artifacts, or inspect releases.
Observe and control GitHub Actions runs — list, view, watch, rerun, logs. Use to check CI status, follow a run live, read failure logs, or re-trigger a failed run.
Binary-search the history to find the commit that introduced a regression. Use to locate exactly when a bug or behavior change first appeared.
Create, switch, list, delete, rename, and track branches. Use to manage local branches or move HEAD between them.
Apply or undo individual commits — cherry-pick a commit onto HEAD, or revert one with an inverse commit. Use to port a fix between branches or back out a change.
Read the history — log, commit graph, show a commit, blame lines, and the reflog. Use to inspect what changed, when, by whom, and to find lost commits.
Replay commits onto a new base, edit history interactively, or autosquash fixups. Use to linearize, reorder, squash, or reword commits before sharing.
Undo and recovery — reflog, ORIG_HEAD, git restore, reset --hard/--soft, and getting back a dropped stash or a lost commit. Use when a change was lost or a command went wrong.
The ref and revision grammar — HEAD, branches, tags, ~/^ ancestry, .. and ... ranges, refspecs, and @{...} reflog syntax. The vocabulary every git tool takes as an argument.
Shelve and restore uncommitted changes — push, pop, list, show, drop, apply a stash. Use to park work-in-progress before switching branches or pulling.
Check out multiple branches at once in separate directories backed by one repo. Use to build, review, or hotfix a branch without disturbing the current tree.
Thin REST helper over curl — call any method on a URL with a bearer token and JSON body, or upload a multipart form. Use to hit an authenticated JSON API or post a file as form data.
curl flag reference — methods, headers, bodies, auth, output, redirects, retries, and the scripting flags (-fsS). Pull when building or reading any curl command line.
Fetch a URL with curl — read the body, dump only the response headers, do a HEAD probe, or save the resource to disk. Use to read or download an HTTP resource without mutating the remote.
Send a body to a URL with curl — POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE with raw data and custom headers, or a json helper that sets the Content-Type. Use to create, replace, update, or delete a remote resource.
HTTP method semantics (safe, idempotent) and status-code families with the common codes. Pull when choosing a method or reading a response code.
How an order total is computed and applied. Pull when implementing checkout, cart, or pricing.
The canonical Product shape (id, name, price). Pull when implementing anything that touches a product line item.
Session and login flow sketch — credentials in, session out. Pull when implementing auth, login, or token refresh.
Cache read-through with TTL expiry. Pull when implementing a cache, memoization, or stale-while-revalidate.
Slice a list into pages of fixed size. Pull when implementing pagination, cursor lists, or batched iteration.
The Result type — success or a typed error, never both. Pull when implementing fallible operations, error mapping, or retry boundaries.
Find files and directories by name with fd — match a name pattern, filter by extension or type, run a command per result. Use to locate paths fast, respecting .gitignore.
Query and transform JSON with jq — apply a filter, list top-level keys, extract a path, pretty-print or emit raw values. Use to slice JSON output or files on the command line.
The jq filter language — identity, field access, iteration, pipes, select/map, to_entries, length, string interpolation, and the -r/-c/--arg flags. The grammar reference behind the jq tool.

How matching works across rg and fd — Rust-regex syntax, where it differs from vim regex, fd smart-case and glob mode, and the walk flags (--hidden, --no-ignore, -g) shared by both. The matching companion to the search tools.
Search file contents with ripgrep — find matches, list matching files, count, preview a replacement, filter by type or glob. Use to grep a repo fast, respecting .gitignore.

How ~/.ssh/config and the SSH client resolve a host — Host aliases, HostName/User/Port/IdentityFile/ProxyJump, known_hosts, agent forwarding, and the -i/-p/-J/-o flags. The reference every ssh tool's host argument resolves against.
Copy files over SSH with scp — push a local path to a remote host, pull a remote path down, or copy between two remotes. Use for one-shot file transfers over an SSH connection.
Manage SSH keys — generate an ed25519 keypair, install a public key on a remote host, add a key to the agent, or list local keys. Use to set up or inspect key-based authentication.
Run things over SSH — execute one command on a remote host, open an interactive shell, or set up a port-forward tunnel. Use to drive a remote machine without leaving the local shell.
Sync directories over SSH with rsync — push a local tree to a remote, pull one down, or mirror so the destination matches the source exactly. Use for incremental, repeatable transfers.

Process text by fields with awk — run a program over stdin or a file, print chosen columns, filter rows by pattern, or sum a column. Use for column-aware extraction and quick aggregation in a pipeline.
awk programming reference — the pattern/action model, records and fields, BEGIN/END, variables, arrays, printf, and common one-liners. Pull when writing an awk program for the @text/awk tool.

Slice and reshape text with the small coreutils — cut columns by field or character, paste files side by side, and translate, delete, or squeeze characters with tr. Use for lightweight pipeline plumbing that needs no full sed/awk program.

Stream-edit text with sed — substitute, delete, or print lines from stdin or a file, or rewrite a file in place. Use to apply a sed script in a pipeline or do a scripted find-and-replace across files.
sed scripting reference — addresses, commands, substitute flags, and the BRE-vs-ERE regex dialect. Pull when writing or reading a sed script for the @text/sed tool.
tmux's server/client model, the `-t session:window.pane` target syntax, and the shape of a `tmux <command>` invocation. Read to address the right pane from the command line.
Configuring tmux — `set-option`/`setw`, `bind-key`/`unbind`, the `.tmux.conf` file, and reloading it live. Read to change tmux behaviour or rebind keys.
The prefix key (`C-b`) and the default in-app keybinding table — windows, panes, copy-mode, sessions. Read to drive tmux interactively from inside a session.
Manage tmux panes within a window — split, select, resize, zoom, kill, layout. Use to tile a window into multiple shells and arrange them.
Drive a live program in a tmux pane — type keys, send literal text, run a shell command, and read back the pane's output. The control surface for scripting a real terminal.
Manage tmux sessions — create, attach, list, rename, kill. Use to start or tear down a named workspace the server keeps alive in the background.
Manage tmux windows within a session — create, select, rename, kill, list, move. Use to organize a session into tabbed windows.
Stage a scope and write a conventional commit. Use when the user asks to commit changes with a message.
Run database migrations and local DB tooling (migrate, rollback, seed, studio, reset). Use when the user asks to migrate, seed, or open the DB.
Build and run the project in Docker (build, up, down, logs, shell). Use when the user asks to containerize, run locally, or debug the container.
Format source files, or check formatting without writing. Use when the user asks to pretty-print or verify style.
Run the project's test/type/lint gate. Use before any release or merge to check the tree is green.
Scaffold a new .jd tool file from a template. Use when the user asks to create, add, or bootstrap a new justdown tool.
Cut and publish a release. Use when the user asks to ship a version (patch/minor/major).
Generate a report file (changelog, dep audit, file tree). Use when the user asks for a written report or exported summary.
Capture a URL to a PNG or PDF file. Use when the user asks to screenshot, capture, or render a page to an image.
Start the dev server (Vite) and keep it running. Use when the user asks to run, start, or serve the app locally.
Apply the same headless vim edit across every file matching a glob, in place. Use for a project-wide substitute, deletion, or ex script with no UI.
The vim/nvim command-line surface — the binary family and every flag that matters for opening, scripting, diffing, and remote-controlling the editor.
Vim insert-mode completion — the Ctrl-x submode (keywords, lines, files, tags, omni), popup navigation, and spelling suggestions. Built-in completion, no plugins.
Configuring vim/nvim — options with :set, key mappings, leader keys, variables, and autocommands. How to change editor behavior from a vimrc or at runtime.
Open files side by side in vim diff mode (vimdiff), including a three-way merge or a file against its last commit. Use to review or resolve differences in vim.
Edit a file in place with vim, headlessly — apply ex commands, a substitute, a deletion, or a script with no UI. Use to script vim edits non-interactively.
Ex / command-line commands — ranges, :global, :substitute, :normal, :argdo/:bufdo, sort, move/copy, read/write. The scriptable command layer vim automation is built from.
Vim folding — collapse and expand regions of a buffer by method (manual, indent, marker, syntax, expr) and the z-key commands that open, close, and navigate folds.
Vim modes — normal, insert, visual, command-line, replace, and terminal — how to enter and leave each, and the keys that only live inside them.
Vim motions — every way to move the cursor or define a range, from character steps to searches, marks, and jumps. The vocabulary operators act on.
The Neovim-specific surface — Lua config and the vim.* modules, the nvim_* API, msgpack-RPC control, and built-in LSP/treesitter/diagnostics. Where nvim goes beyond stock Vim.
Launch vim with the right invocation — at a line, in tabs or splits, read-only, restoring a session, or with no config. Use to open files in vim the way the task needs.
Vim operators and text objects — the operator + motion/text-object grammar (delete, change, yank, indent…) and dot-repeat that make editing composable.
Vim quickfix and location lists — populate from :make/:grep/:vimgrep, jump through results, and batch-edit every entry with :cdo. The result-navigation surface.
Vim registers, marks, and macros — where yanked/deleted text goes, named positions, and recording keystroke sequences to replay.
Control a running vim/nvim instance from outside — start a server, send keystrokes, evaluate expressions, open files in it. Use to drive a live editor programmatically.
Run a vimscript or a Lua script through nvim headlessly. Use to execute editor automation that lives in a .vim or .lua file.
Vim search and regex — the pattern syntax, magic levels, anchors, classes, and replacement specials used by /search, :substitute, and :global.
Vim tag-based code navigation — jump to a definition with a ctags index, walk the tag stack, and preview tags. The classic go-to-definition surface.
Vim buffers, windows, tabs, and sessions — loading files, splitting the view, moving between viewports, and saving a layout to restore later.
Set up a fresh clone for development end to end. Use when the user says "set this up", "onboard", or "get me running".
End-to-end ship workflow — gate, release, and announce. Use when the user says "ship it" and the tree should go out end to end.
